Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.

Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: Looking at the image, the player moved LEFT. The white strip with blue center is still above the player. Let me check the exact positions.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check current state after LEFT
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    b = n['boundary']
    rows = [p[0] for p in b]
    cols = [p[1] for p in b]
    if n['color'] not in ['w', 'g']:
        print(f"id={n['id']}, color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, bbox=({min(rows)},{min(cols)})-({max(rows)},{max(cols)}), children={n['children']}")

# Check what changed
if last_transition:
    before = last_transition.before_frame.segmentation
    after = last_transition.after_frame.segmentation
    print("\nPlayer before:", [n for n in before['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'N'][0]['boundary'])
    print("Player after:", [n for n in after['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'N'][0]['boundary'])
    print("White strip before:", [n for n in before['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'W'][0]['boundary'])
    print("White strip after:", [n for n in after['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'W'][0]['boundary'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.content:
OK so after moving LEFT:
- Player moved from (41,32)-(43,35) to (41,28)-(43,31), which is 4 cols left
- White strip moved from (36,32)-(40,35) to (36,28)-(40,31), also 4 cols left

Now the white strip is at (36,28)-(40,31) and the blue bar is at (28,28)-(31,39). The white strip is below the blue bar (rows 36-40 vs rows 28-31), and they overlap in columns 28-31.
The charcoal square at (28,16)-(31,19) is to the left of the blue bar.
Let me think about what the goal might be:
1. Push the white strip into the blue bar
2. Push the white strip into the charcoal square
3. Drop the white strip at a specific location
4. Push the blue bar into a specific position
Given the current state, the white strip is below the blue bar. If I move UP, the white strip will move up and might collide with the blue bar. Let me try moving UP and see what happens.
But wait, I need to be careful about the move limit. The HUD shows 63 pink + 1 charcoal, which might mean I have very few moves left. Let me check the HUD more carefully.
Actually, looking at the HUD bar, it's at row 63 with 63 pink pixels and 1 charcoal pixel. This might represent the remaining moves or the progress. If the pink pixels represent actions taken, then I've taken 63 actions (out of 64). But I've taken 205 actions, so this doesn't match.
Let me just focus on the gameplay and try to solve the puzzle. I'll move UP to see if the white strip collides with the blue bar.

</parameter>
